topic: IO - Async File IO with tokio::fs
content: When you switch from blocking std::fs to async file operations using tokio::fs, the API looks almost identical to its synchronous counterpart, which is a deliberate design choice by the Tokio team to minimize cognitive load. You can immediately write tokio::fs::read_file('config.toml') and it will return a future that yields a String once complete, or use tokio::fs::write_file(path, content) to append data without worrying about complex thread management. The core distinction lies in the underlying implementation: std::fs operations block the calling thread until completion, whereas tokio::fs delegates file I/O to an async runtime using io::AsyncRead and io::AsyncWrite traits under the hood. This allows your application to perform other work while waiting for disk access, but it introduces a specific gotcha that trips up even experienced developers: race conditions on file metadata during concurrent operations. Consider a scenario where you have two tasks reading from the same log file simultaneously; std::fs::read locks the file exclusively until completion, preventing interleaved data, whereas tokio::fs::read_file operates concurrently by default unless explicitly configured otherwise with tokio::fs::File and the File::lock_async method. If you attempt to write overlapping content without proper synchronization using tokio::sync::Mutex or atomic counters, you risk corrupting your log entries or overwriting critical configuration data. Another subtle pitfall involves file creation: while std::fs creates a new empty file silently if it doesn't exist, async operations can fail unpredictably depending on the operating system and whether the parent directory exists; for instance, calling tokio::fs::create_dir_all('nested/path/to/file.txt') will succeed only if the runtime has already established the necessary permissions and the path is valid. You must be explicit about error handling because async file creation does not automatically propagate failures like std::fs does in certain edge cases involving permission denied errors on macOS or Windows. Finally, remember that tokio::fs functions are safe to use with tokio::spawn but require careful handling of cancellation tokens; if your process is killed mid-write using tokio::sync::mpsc channel shutdown, the underlying OS file descriptor may be left in a partially written state requiring fsync calls via std::fs::File to ensure durability. The best practice pattern is to wrap all async file operations in try blocks with explicit error propagation to the application level rather than letting them fail silently, ensuring your logs reflect exactly what happened on disk without guessing.
